title: Fitness Activity Coach — Evaluation Rules
source: MASS Research Review Vol 9-10, Phase 1-3 coaching program for Jason Tilley. Extended with Bodybuilding & Nutrition Knowledge Base (2026).

nutrition.fail: Protein at {daily_protein}g — below {target_protein_min}g target. Try adding an extra serving at post-gym meal or dinner.
nutrition.evidence: ISSN Position Stand (2017): 1.6-2.2 g/kg/day for athletes. Refalo/Trexler/Helms (2025): 2.0 g/kg ideal in deficit. Trexler MASS V10I7: 'Diminishing returns above ~1.6 g/kg/day, 2.0-2.2 safe for deficit.'

nutrition.fail: Calories at {daily_calories}. Target ~{target_calories}. {daily_calories > target_calories_max ? 'Too high: tighten dinner portion or skip extras.' : 'Too low: risk of muscle loss in deficit >500 cal/day.'}
nutrition.evidence: Helms MASS V10I7: '~135 kcal/day deficit produces body recomposition. Deficit >500 kcal/day limits muscle gain.' Koehler & Murphy (2022) meta-regression: deficit up to 150 kcal/day still permits lean mass gain.

nutrition.fail: Pre-gym snack at {pre_gym_snack_time}. Aim ~45 min before training ({training_time}).
nutrition.evidence: Van Loon (2011): Pre-exercise protein timing affects amino acid availability during and after training.

nutrition.pass: Post-gym meal at {post_gym_meal_time} — within MPS peak window ✓
nutrition.fail: Post-gym meal at {post_gym_meal_time} — beyond 60 min post-exercise. Earlier is better for MPS.
nutrition.evidence: ISSN Position Stand on Nutrient Timing (2017): Pre- and post-exercise nutrition supports MPS. Schoenfeld et al. (2013): Total daily protein > timing precision.

nutrition.fail: 3pm snack MISSED. This is the #1 candy craving intervention. Protein + fiber at 3pm prevents 4pm glucose crash that triggers reactive sugar eating. Log it tomorrow.
nutrition.evidence: Paddon-Jones et al. (2008): Protein improves satiety and glucose regulation. Hägele et al. (2025, Nat Metab): High-protein meals stabilize postprandial glucose.

nutrition.fail: Casein MISSED at 8pm. Without it: blood glucose drifts down around 3am → cortisol spike → you wake up. This is the single most impactful intervention for your sleep quality and the 3am wake. Set a phone alarm: 'CASEIN NOW' at 8pm.
nutrition.evidence: Trommelen & van Loon (2016): Pre-sleep casein increases overnight MPS by ~22%. Res et al. (2012, J Nutr): Pre-sleep protein before sleep stimulates MPS overnight. Helms MASS V10I7: Body recomposition article discusses glucose counter-regulation mechanism.

nutrition.description: Family dinner — single plate, no seconds
nutrition.fail: {dinner_plates} plates at dinner. Target: 1 plate. Protein + veg first, carbs last. Wait 10min before deciding if you need more.
nutrition.evidence: Program custom rule based on analysis of Jason's untracked restaurant dinners being the key variable in 4-month stall.

nutrition.description: Dinner — protein and veg before carbs
nutrition.fail: Eat protein and vegetables before touching carbs/starch at dinner. This naturally reduces total intake without tracking.
nutrition.evidence: Paddon-Jones (2008): Protein first improves satiety signaling.

nutrition.pass: No candy cravings ✓ — 3pm snack doing its job
nutrition.fail: {candy_cravings_today} candy craving episodes. 3pm snack should prevent these. Did you eat the 3pm snack? If yes, try increasing protein at lunch or adding 5g fiber to the 3pm snack.
nutrition.evidence: Kohanmoo et al. (2020, Physiol Behav): Short- and long-term protein consumption reduces appetite and appetite-regulating gastrointestinal hormones.

nutrition.fail: No pre-workout fuel logged. 1-2 g/kg carbohydrate in the 1-2 hours before training supports session quality.
nutrition.evidence: Henselmans et al. (2022, Nutrients): carb pre-workout supports training quality.

nutrition.description: Protein across 4-6 meals, ~0.4 g/kg per meal
nutrition.fail: Only {meals_count} meals. Target 4-6 at 32-45g protein each. Even distribution sustains MPS.

nutrition.description: Daily fat >= 0.5 g/kg for hormone function
nutrition.fail: Fat at {daily_fat_g}g - below 46g minimum. Inadequate fat impairs testosterone. Minimum 0.5 g/kg/day.
nutrition.evidence: Helms et al. (2014, JISSN): 'Fat floor of ~0.5 g/kg/day for hormone function.'

training.fail: Training MISSED on {day}. Scheduled 11am at commercial gym. If recovering from illness or excessive fatigue, rest is OK — otherwise, progress requires consistency.
training.evidence: Schoenfeld (2010): Training consistency is the primary driver of long-term hypertrophy. Helms MASS V10I7: 'Body recomposition relies on a strong anabolic stimulus and shifting fuel use to body fat.'

training.description: Total weekly sets per muscle group (12-16 range)
training.pass: Volume per muscle: {weekly_sets_per_muscle} sets — within 12-16 target ✓
training.fail: Volume per muscle: {weekly_sets_per_muscle} sets. Target 12-16. {weekly_sets_per_muscle < 12 ? 'Not enough stimulus for growth. Add an extra exercise or set.' : 'Excessive for deficit — may impair recovery. Drop 1-2 sets.'}
training.evidence: Schoenfeld, Ogborn, Krieger (2017, PMID 27433992): Graded dose-response from <5 sets/week → 5-9 → 10+. 10+ sets/week/muscle produces superior hypertrophy. Schoenfeld (2010): Higher volume relationships weaken when equated for frequency.

training.fail: Compounds at RIR {compound_rir}. Target 1-2 RIR on compounds. In a deficit, excessive proximity to failure increases fatigue without additional hypertrophy benefit.
training.evidence: Grgic, Schoenfeld et al. (2022, PMID 33497853): Failure NOT required for hypertrophy. 0-2 RIR produces equal gains. Refalo/Trexler/Helms (2025): In a deficit, excessive fatigue impairs recovery.

training.fail: Isolations at RIR {isolation_rir}. Target 0-1. Isolations are safer to push to failure since they don't tax the CNS as heavily.
training.evidence: Program design principle. Helms MASS V10I7: Biceps respond to high volume + proximity to failure.

training.fail: Double progression: add reps before adding weight. If you increased weight but dropped below the rep range, back off. Hit the top of the rep range for 2 sessions before adding 5%.
training.evidence: Helms et al. (2020): Progressive overload is the primary driver of hypertrophy. Double progression is the standard implementation for body recomposition phases.

training.description: No barbell squats — use knee-safe alternatives
training.fail: Barbell squat logged. Your knee pain history indicates leg press + Bulgarian split squat + leg extension as safer alternatives.
training.evidence: Program custom rule based on Jason's knee pain history and self-correction in training log. Comerford & Mottram (2012): Movement screening and exercise selection for joint health.

training.description: No barbell OHP — use DB neutral grip press
training.fail: Barbell OHP logged. Your shoulder pain history indicates DB neutral-grip press as a safer alternative. Consider reducing weight.
training.evidence: Program custom rule based on Jason's shoulder pain.

training.fail: Face pulls only {weekly_face_pulls}x this week. Target 2x. External rotation work prevents shoulder impingement and balances all the pressing.
training.evidence: Kibler et al. (2013): Scapular muscle balance important for shoulder health. Face pulls target external rotation and scapular retraction.

training.fail: Rest too short. 2-3 min for compounds, 1.5-2 for isolation. Short rests limit per-set volume.
training.evidence: Schoenfeld (2016, JSCR): '3-min rest superior to 1-min for hypertrophy.' ISSN position stand.

training.description: Deload every 4-6 weeks when volume plateaus
training.fail: {weeks_since_deload} weeks without a deload. After 4-6 weeks of progression, take a deload week at 50-60% volume to dissipate fatigue.
training.evidence: Williams et al. (2019, Front Physiol): Periodized programs help manage fatigue.

training.fail: Session {session_minutes} min. Target 60-90 min. Longer sessions increase cortisol without additional hypertrophic benefit.
training.evidence: Hackney & Viru (2011): Prolonged exercise elevates cortisol and blunts MPS.

sleep.fail: Caffeine at {last_caffeine_time} — PAST 2pm cutoff. Caffeine half-life at 47 is ~6 hours. Caffeine at 2:30pm = 50% still active at 8:30pm, 25% at 11pm. That's enough to disrupt deep sleep even if you fall asleep. Cut off at 2pm sharp.
sleep.evidence: Drake et al. (2013, J Clin Sleep Med): Caffeine 6 hours before bed significantly disrupts sleep. At age 47, caffeine clearance is slower — half-life averages 6 hours vs 4.5 at 25.

sleep.fail: Bedtime {bedtime} — outside 10:00 ± 30 min window. Consistent bedtimes improve sleep quality and cortisol rhythm.
sleep.evidence: Buysse (2014): Sleep consistency is a key component of sleep health. Irregular bedtimes disrupt circadian rhythms.
Consistent wake time reinforces circadian phase.

sleep.fail: Woke at {wake_time} early. 3am wake = blood glucose dip → cortisol spike. Did you take casein at 8pm? If yes, try increasing casein to 40g or adding a small complex carb. If no: this is why. Start casein tonight.
sleep.evidence: Van Loon (2016): Pre-sleep casein attenuates overnight cortisol spikes by maintaining stable blood glucose. Consitt et al. (2022, J Int Soc Sports Nutr): Protein timing for glucose regulation.

sleep.fail: Screens on at {last_screen_time}. Blue light suppresses melatonin production. Put phone in the kitchen, not the bedroom.
sleep.evidence: Chang et al. (2015, PNAS): Evening blue light exposure suppresses melatonin and shifts circadian rhythms. Higuchi et al. (2005): Older adults are more sensitive to blue light suppression.

sleep.fail: Sleep quality: {sleep_quality}/5. {sleep_quality <= 2 ? 'Poor sleep. Check: caffeine after 2pm? Casein taken? Screens before bed? Stress?' : 'Below target. Check the 3 pillars: caffeine cutoff, casein, screens.'}
sleep.evidence: Sleep quality is the primary recovery marker in the program.

supplements.fail: Creatine MISSED. 5g/day consistent — no loading or cycling needed. Take at any consistent time.
supplements.evidence: ISSN Position Stand on Creatine (2017, PMC5469049): 5g/day creatine monohydrate is the A+ supplement for strength and hypertrophy.

supplements.fail: Vitamin D MISSED. 3,000 IU with breakfast. Deficiency risk higher at 28% BF (sequestered in adipose).
supplements.evidence: Holick et al. (2011): Vitamin D deficiency common at higher body fat. 2,000-4,000 IU/day for adults.

supplements.fail: Magnesium MISSED. 200-400mg magnesium glycinate 30min before bed → better sleep quality.
supplements.evidence: Abbasi et al. (2012): Magnesium supplementation improves sleep quality in elderly adults. Glycinate form has highest bioavailability.

supplements.fail: Beta-alanine missed. 4-5 g/day increases intramuscular carnosine, buffers H+ during high-rep sets.
supplements.evidence: ISSN position stand (2015): '4-6 g/day beta-alanine for 2-4 weeks elevates carnosine.'

supplements.fail: Omega-3 missed. 2-3 g EPA+DHA/day supports recovery, inflammation modulation, and may preserve lean mass during deficit.
supplements.evidence: Smith et al. (2011, AJCN): 'Fish oil supports MPS in older adults.' McGlory & Phillips (2016): Anti-catabolic in deficit.

supplements.fail: Caffeine {caffeine_mg_per_kg} mg/kg {last_caffeine_time >= '14:00' ? 'after 2pm - disrupts sleep' : 'above 6 mg/kg - no additional benefit'}. Target 3-6 mg/kg, 60 min pre-workout, none after 2pm.
supplements.evidence: Guest et al. (2021, JISSN): '3-6 mg/kg caffeine 60 min pre-exercise.' Grgic (2025): 'Min effective dose ~2-3 mg/kg.'

recovery.pass: Weight change: {weekly_weight_change_lbs} lb this week — in range ✓
recovery.fail: Weight change: {weekly_weight_change_lbs} lb/week. Target -0.3 to -0.6 lb/week. {weekly_weight_change < -0.8 ? 'Too fast — deficit too large. Add 150-200 cal/day.' : 'Too slow — tighten dinner portion or reduce 100 cal. Wait 3 weeks before adjusting.'}
recovery.evidence: Helms MASS V10I7: Body recomposition requires modest deficit (135-356 cal/day). Fast weight loss = muscle loss.

recovery.fail: Waist unchanged or up this week. If weight is stable but waist is the same or larger after 3+ weeks, deficit may need adjustment or dinner system needs tightening.
recovery.evidence: Waist circumference is the primary recomposition metric — it tracks fat loss independent of muscle gain (which may mask scale changes).

recovery.fail: Energy: {energy_score}/5. Low energy + poor sleep + dropping performance = deficit too large or recovery insufficient. Consider diet break (maintenance for 1-2 weeks).
recovery.evidence: Trexler et al. (2014, JISSN): Metabolic adaptation markers include lethargy and reduced performance.

recovery.fail: Stress: {stress_score}/5. High stress increases cortisol, impairs recovery, and makes body recomposition harder. Consider 10-min walk, breathing exercise, or a deload day.
recovery.evidence: Stults-Kolehmainen & Bartholomew (2012): Psychological stress impairs recovery from resistance training.

recovery.fail: Hunger: {hunger_score}/5. High hunger = deficit may be too aggressive. Add 100-200 cal (priority: protein or vegetables for volume). Check 3pm snack compliance.
recovery.evidence: Helms et al. (2014): Hunger management is critical for diet adherence.

recovery.fail: Performance DROPPING 2+ reps across all lifts for 2 weeks. Possible causes: deficit too large, insufficient sleep, accumulated fatigue. Action: deload week OR add 200 cal/day for 1 week and monitor.
recovery.evidence: Zourdos et al. (2023): Performance drops across multiple lifts signal accumulated fatigue. Helms: 'If everything is going wrong at once, it's recovery, not the program.'

recovery.fail: Sleep duration {sleep_hours} hrs. Target 7-9 hrs. <7 hrs increases cortisol ~21%, drops testosterone ~24%, and reduces MPS ~18% per single night of deprivation.
recovery.evidence: Pisanu et al. (2025, JCM): 'Sleep restriction 5-6 hrs/night increases cortisol, decreases anabolic hormones.' Lamon et al. (2021, Physiol Rep): '24% testosterone drop, 18% MPS reduction from single deprivation night.'

recovery.description: Exercises performed through full range of motion
recovery.fail: Partial ROM detected. Full ROM, especially through the lengthened/stretched position, produces superior hypertrophy. Control the eccentric through 2-4 seconds.
recovery.evidence: Wolf et al. (2023): 'Full ROM > partial for hypertrophy.' Ottinger et al. (2025): 'Lengthened partials comparable to full ROM.'

weekly.fail: Weekly compliance: {weekly_compliance_pct}%. Target ≥80%. {weekly_compliance_pct < 60 ? 'Significant gaps — identify the blockers and fix one at a time.' : 'Near target. One area to focus on next week.'}
weekly.evidence: Adherence is the strongest predictor of long-term results in exercise science.

weekly.fail: Your top priority this week: {weakest_area}. {weakest_area == 'casein' ? 'Missing casein correlates perfectly with 3am wake-ups. Set a phone alarm at 8pm saying CASEIN NOW.' : weakest_area == '3pm_snack' ? 'The 3pm snack is your candy craving intervention. Prep them on Sunday for the week.' : weakest_area == 'caffeine' ? 'No caffeine after 2pm. Switch to herbal tea or decaf.' : weakest_area == 'training' ? 'You missed {missed_sessions} sessions this week. Aim for 5/5 next week.' : weakest_area == 'protein' ? 'Aim for 200g/day. Start lunch with protein.' : 'Keep doing what you did this week.'}
